extracts.extract: We are the best advocates for our journalistic needs. It's critical that newsrooms take a beat and think through what their needs are, instead of trusting vendors to tell us what our needs are.
extracts.chapter: AI-driven tools, tips and tricks to empower your data journalism
extracts.notes: Framing: journalists should define own needs before evaluating AI tools

extracts.extract: GitHub Copilot plugin for VSCode helps me scaffold whole Svelte app projects on the fly. I designed an entire internal Minneapolis crime tracking dashboard in R using only GPT4 prompts.
extracts.chapter: AI-driven tools, tips and tricks to empower your data journalism
extracts.notes: Hargarten example: LLMs for scaffolding entire analysis/viz projects

extracts.extract: Any of the LLM tools can generate near-flawless FOIA or state data practices requests in the correct format, and a few times even found and appended my contact information.
extracts.chapter: AI-driven tools, tips and tricks to empower your data journalism
extracts.notes: Hargarten: LLMs drafting public records requests with correct legal citations

extracts.extract: It's not in a place where it can automagically just do all your work for you (yet). It's very good at coding and solving tech challenges, but soulless-to-suspect around much else. Our journalistic sensibilities are necessary to guide these tools.
extracts.chapter: AI-driven tools, tips and tricks to empower your data journalism
extracts.notes: Hargarten: AI shifts focus from nuts-and-bolts to accuracy, creativity, storytelling

extracts.extract: Manual checking of data integrity is so, so important and often ignored. Problems can be very specific to the problem and they arise very unexpectedly. So always assuming there could be something off about the tool is a good attitude.
extracts.chapter: AI-driven tools, tips and tricks to empower your data journalism
extracts.notes: June Kim: default assumption that AI output may contain errors

extracts.extract: Don't be AFRAID to experiment and fail. Just don't publish anything unless it has human editing/quality control on the back end. (AP's 80/20 rule)
extracts.chapter: AI-driven tools, tips and tricks to empower your data journalism
extracts.notes: Reilley: AP 80/20 rule -- AI drafts, humans verify before publication

extracts.extract: Prompting is an art all by itself. Depending upon the tool, there can be wildly different results between asking for the same thing one way versus another. More detail is important, but not so much that it crosses and confuses your asks.
extracts.chapter: AI-driven tools, tips and tricks to empower your data journalism
extracts.notes: Hargarten: prompt craft as distinct skill; balance detail vs. confusion

extracts.extract: Understanding how the tools are built and being diligent about learning and communicating the limitations of the tool/methodologies. Very flawed. A lot of work to ask people to do, but there's just no way around it.
extracts.chapter: AI-driven tools, tips and tricks to empower your data journalism
extracts.notes: June Kim: journalists must understand and communicate AI tool limitations

extracts.extract: Embrace being lazy but with caution... LLMs hallucinate, get things wrong frequently, and are non-deterministic. You already know what the answer should look like. 80% of your time is spent on easy but time-consuming tasks. Save time/energy for the hard problems that require your thinking, judgment and expertise.
extracts.notes: Kao/Soma: use LLMs for tasks where you already know what correct output looks like

extracts.extract: LLMs can also help with tasks that require basic, commonly known domain knowledge, like: Template code, Basic SQL, Boilerplate data viz (including identifying common approach), Regex, Explaining vague errors and exceptions, Simple line editing (finding headlines or alternative phrasing) (BUT it can water-down nuanced/complex thoughts)
extracts.notes: Taxonomy of LLM-suitable tasks for data reporters

extracts.extract: LLMs are generating statistically likely tokens to imitate people based on context. They have no internal monologue - they need to compute out loud. They can't go back and edit what they randomly generated, even if it's wrong. BUT they have perfect working memory (of what's already written down).
extracts.notes: Kao: understanding LLM mechanics helps journalists use them more effectively

extracts.extract: Provide outside knowledge: 'I googled _____ and found'. Work iteratively. Let the model talk\! 'State the assumptions and then...' 'Work step-by-step' 'Critique your reasoning/conclusions'. Unlock parts of the LLM that might have better abilities: 'As an expert...'
extracts.notes: Practical prompting strategies: supply context, step-by-step, role assignment

extracts.extract: You are good at what the model isn't, e.g.: define the goal, break down the task, logic/creativity/metacognition
extracts.notes: Humans define goals and decompose tasks; LLMs execute rote work

extracts.extract: Terms and conditions keep changing -- don't know at what point your data, prompts, and responses will be used to improve existing commercial products like ChatGPT. Sensitive documents or datasets -- you might not feel comfortable relying on a third-party entity. Can deploy to an air-gapped computer if needed.
extracts.notes: Bengani: rationale for running LLMs locally vs. cloud for sensitive journalism

extracts.extract: Test the same thing over and over again to see how varied and inconsistent the results can be. Check the context overflow setting and ensure you handle that by setting a higher context length, chunking your documents to ensure there's no overflow, ensuring third-party libraries haven't specified different max token lengths.
extracts.notes: Bengani: repeated testing reveals non-determinism; manage context overflow

extracts.extract: Just because an LLM might be able to do something, it doesn't mean it's the right approach. Large language models are awful at everything they do and it's only by a strange, strange accident that we find them useful.
extracts.notes: Soma: LLM capability does not equal suitability; critical evaluation needed

extracts.extract: Error resilient tasks: Problems with tight feedback loops. A large number of tasks where individual errors don't matter too much. Tasks where low-quality outputs can just be ignored.
extracts.notes: Soma: LLMs best for error-resilient tasks with tight feedback loops

extracts.extract: Errors are going to happen, so anticipate and measure them. Take a large handful, evaluate with a human. Out of 4 scored by a human as YES, our AI marked 3 as YES and one as MAYBE. Out of the 5 scored as YES by the AI, the human only agreed on 3 of them.
extracts.notes: Soma: sample-and-validate approach; confusion matrix for AI output quality

extracts.extract: Don't trust the answers. Measure the errors\! (Confusion matrices + error tracking). Don't trust the output. Validate and repair\! (Guardrails). Don't trust the bot. Provide suggestions that humans can ignore instead of demands\!
extracts.notes: Soma: three-part LLM validation framework -- measure, validate/repair, human override

extracts.extract: CAPTCHAs are a common way to prevent bots from accessing a website. They are sometimes used by government entities to make it harder for journalists to access public information. There are three primary strategies to overcoming CAPTCHAs: Find a way to never have to solve a CAPTCHA. Solve the CAPTCHA programatically with some type of Optical Character Recognition. Solve the CAPTCHA with a human worker.
extracts.chapter: Automated CAPTCHA solving with Anti-Captcha API
extracts.notes: Little: three strategies for overcoming CAPTCHAs blocking public data access

extracts.extract: The sitekey is not always this easy to find. Some custom implementations purposely hide the sitekey to make it more difficult to solve the CAPTCHA. In these cases, you may need to use a more advanced method to find the sitekey.
extracts.chapter: Automated CAPTCHA solving with Anti-Captcha API
extracts.notes: Little: sitekeys may be hidden in JavaScript; inspect source code

extracts.extract: Using the tools described by Leon Yin's Finding Undocumented APIs to find an API that has the information we need. It is possible to find an API that appears to be protected by a CAPTCHA but it's actually not. Other times, the API requires a CAPTCHA solution to be sent as a parameter.
extracts.chapter: Automated CAPTCHA solving with Anti-Captcha API
extracts.notes: Little: undocumented APIs can sometimes bypass CAPTCHA entirely

extracts.extract: Import by range. Use janitor to help with unwieldy names. Append a prefix for certain columns to name. Convert bad Excel numbers to true numbers. Overwrite names if you need to.
extracts.notes: R workflow for wrangling messy UN population spreadsheet

extracts.extract: Not opening a CSV through import can truncate data starting with a zero such as Northeastern zip codes or ID numbers. We do not want to change the data itself.
extracts.chapter: Google Sheets: Importing and data prep
extracts.notes: Garcia: always use import function to preserve leading zeros in zip codes/IDs

extracts.extract: =importHTML("URL", "table", 1). URL = The URL of the webpage you are trying to scrape. table = This parameter takes two options: table or list. 1 = the number of the table on the page.
extracts.chapter: Google Sheets: Importing and data prep
extracts.notes: Garcia: importHTML function for pulling static HTML tables into Sheets

extracts.extract: API stands for Application Programming Interface. It enables two software applications to communicate with each other. REST stands for Representational State Transfer. Data exchange between the client and the server is based on the use of HTTP, which allows you to type in your client requests in your own web browser as your regular URLs.
extracts.notes: Nguyen: foundational explanation of REST API data collection for journalists

extracts.extract: Most of the time, your need won't be satisfied if you just rely on an HTTP url to collect small chunks of data. You need to use some Python/R code to help with authentication and pagination. To do so, you need to harness THREE basic tools: Google Colab: a coding environment. ChatGPT: a bot that can teach you some Python code. Dev Tools: web browser's developer tools that allow you to inspect websites, track API requests, and analyze API responses.
extracts.notes: Nguyen: three-tool stack for advanced API data collection

extracts.extract: Open the website on Chrome browser and turn on Dev Tools. Monitor the Network tab, especially the fetch/XHR section, where some API requests may show up. Find the cURL under the Name column. Convert cURL to Python code.
extracts.notes: Nguyen: using browser DevTools to discover hidden APIs and convert to Python

extracts.extract: Found and reverse-engineered underlying APIs powering each search portal. Built four scrapers using similar set of sequential requests: Autocomplete and verify address, Choose apartment number, Check availability and list plans. Only made possible by using a session to keep track of cookies and params. Scraped ~100x addresses at once by making each scraper asynchronous. Circumvented IP blocking with a proxy.
extracts.notes: Yin: Still Loading project -- async scraping with session/cookie management

extracts.extract: Don't repeat yourself: Structured naming system for outputs. Make a todo list: AWS SQS (a queuing service) is useful for keeping tabs on what's to be done. Save receipts: Keep raw data, don't parse until later. Circumvent blocking: Use proxies as a last resort. Different levels of proxies.
extracts.notes: Yin: bookkeeping tips for large scraping projects -- save raw, parse later

extracts.extract: Used OpenAddresses and NYC Open Data to collect 12M addresses from 45 cities. Census Geocoder to merge incorporated city and block group. Goal: Collect plans for 10% of addresses in each census block group (stratified sample) to collect representative samples in each city.
extracts.notes: Yin: stratified sampling at block-group level for representative coverage

extracts.extract: AWS -> elastic container service = break down the problem into bite-sized chunks. Large scale projects are all about breaking things down, and thinking a series of functions/inputs/outputs. Write a scraper to scrape one page at a time, and funnel documents to an s3 bucket. Write a script that takes a start and end case number, scrapes those cases.
extracts.notes: Mahajan: decompose large scraping into smallest unit of work; container per task

extracts.extract: Build it step by step: Local computer => Local docker image => One cloud instance => Multiple cloud instances. Separate scraping and analysis. Collect data in as raw a state as possible. Back-of-the-envelope math. Manage time, money and time spent building.
extracts.notes: Kao: progressive scaling from local to cloud; separate collection from analysis

extracts.extract: Look at the cost structure of your tools; design your system to save money. Compute: Fargate vs EC2 - Fargate is about 20% more expensive, but it shuts down when it finishes the task. Storage: S3 (cost per transaction: directory listing vs retrieving a file; ongoing storage costs). Turn on cloud resources only when you're using them\!
extracts.notes: Kao: practical cloud cost management for news organizations

extracts.extract: Scheduling: Use cron (locally) or configure cloud instances to kick off at set times or intervals. Be discrete: Break tasks up into smallest unit of work. Use queuing to communicate between tasks. Keep logs: Record what was done to help troubleshoot. Optimize: Use queues and logs to find bottlenecks.
extracts.notes: General tips for large-scale data collection operations

extracts.extract: Tighten loops, experiment, re-run, change schemas to suit analysis. Tunable run times to parse >100K documents. Hasura (GraphQL) + Observable (Analysis + Data Memos). Up-to-date fact checking.
extracts.notes: Mahajan: iterative analysis workflow with queryable database for large document sets

extracts.extract: Text extraction from PDFs and OCRing image files is much more of an art than a science. The tools below are meant to give you a lot of different options to try to get the text out of an image PDF and into a format that you can work with. Sometimes they will work amazingly well. Sometimes they won't work at all.
extracts.notes: PDF text extraction requires experimentation; no single tool works universally

extracts.extract: I often use this tool to check for hidden text, particularly in documents that have redactions. Lawyers for Paul Manafort accidentally filed a document in court that wasn't properly redacted -- even though the document contained blacked out sections, the text was still present in the document.
extracts.chapter: Scenario 1: Analyzing a computer generated pdf
extracts.notes: pdftotext reveals text under redactions -- powerful verification technique

extracts.extract: The general standard for OCR is 300 dpi, or 300 dots per inch, though ABBYY recommends using 400-600 for font sizes smaller than 10 point. In ImageMagick, this is specified using the density flag.
extracts.chapter: Scenario 3: Combining skills for searchable PDF
extracts.notes: 300 DPI standard for OCR; 400-600 for small fonts; ImageMagick density flag

extracts.extract: convert -density 300 russia_findings.pdf -depth 8 -strip -background white -alpha off russia_findings.tiff. -density ups the dpi to 300. -depth 8 is the number of bits in a color sample. -strip strips off any junk on the file. -background white sets the background to help with contrasting text. -alpha off handles transparency.
extracts.chapter: Scenario 3: Combining skills for searchable PDF
extracts.notes: ImageMagick convert workflow: optimize image for OCR with density, depth, strip, background

extracts.extract: OCR'd PDFs don't often have the lines, curves and rects that pdfplumber uses to parse computer-generated text. Instead, we are using the pixel position of each word and the structure of the PDF to pass in explicit lines where each column should be separated based on our own visual inspection of the document.
extracts.notes: OCR'd PDFs need explicit column lines; use pixel positions from visual inspection

extracts.extract: A major advantage of Jupyter over spreadsheets is that rather than manipulating the data through a haphazard series of clicks and keypunches, we will be gradually grinding it down using a computer programming script that is transparent and reproducible.
extracts.notes: Scripted analysis in Jupyter enables transparent, reproducible data work

extracts.extract: On closer inspection, we can see that Bell 206 helicopter is listed two different ways, as BELL 206 and bell 206. The variation in capitalization is causing pandas to treat them as two distinct values. This is a common problem and a simple example of how dirty data can trip up a computer program.
extracts.notes: value_counts reveals dirty data; str.upper() standardizes capitalization

extracts.extract: In the real world, you will almost always need to clean your data before you can analyze it, though the challenges will typically be more complex than this one. The basic process is always the same: Identify the problem, fix it, and then check your work. The value_counts method is one of the most useful tools in this process.
extracts.notes: Universal cleaning cycle: identify problem, fix, check work

extracts.extract: When you import a CSV file with read_csv it will take a guess at column types -- for example, integer, float, boolean, datetime or string -- but it will default to a generic object type. The to_datetime method included with pandas can handle the conversion.
extracts.notes: pd.to_datetime converts string dates; check info() to verify type changes

extracts.extract: In a typical analysis, you'd import all of your libraries in one cell at the top of the file. That way, if you need to install or make changes to the packages a notebook uses, you know where to find them and you won't hit errors importing a package midway through running a file.
extracts.notes: Notebook hygiene: all imports at top of file

extracts.extract: If we change a variable, future cells that use that variable won't change unless we run them again. This is one reason that it can be good to clear cell outputs and rerun your analysis every so often. If you've been going back and forth editing cells, you may have saved variables in memory that are no longer accurate.
extracts.notes: Restart kernel and rerun to catch stale variable state

extracts.extract: These charts give us plenty of areas where we might want to dig in and ask more questions, but none are polished enough to pop into a news story quite yet. Downloading the file as an SVG will let anyone with graphics software like Adobe Illustrator take this file and tweak the design.
extracts.notes: Export Altair charts as SVG for graphics department polish

extracts.extract: Simplify: Break complex tasks into smaller steps. Use chain-of-thought and step-by-step prompting. Provide training examples to help with new or complex tasks. Add relevant contextual information whenever possible. Repeat the main instructions at the end if need be. Include words or phrases to guide the responses.
extracts.chapter: Hands-on with AI Tools for Journalists
extracts.notes: Reilley: 6 practical prompting rules for journalism workflows

extracts.extract: Use clear punctuation, headings and section markers. Specify the output structure you want (format, lenses, version of software for image generation). Write and rewrite the prompt, and use Regenerate buttons until you get a result you want. Be Crystal Clear: Chat with the AI like you're giving directions to a lost tourist or a new intern.
extracts.chapter: Hands-on with AI Tools for Journalists
extracts.notes: Reilley: specify output format; iterate prompts; treat LLM like intern

extracts.extract: AI is better at creating effective language around information you already have sourced and know to be true. Facts relayed by AI can be effective leads but should be independently confirmed. Similar thinking: You wouldn't quote Wikipedia but you might find the footnote links useful.
extracts.chapter: Hands-on with AI Tools for Journalists
extracts.notes: Reilley: AI facts = leads not sources; treat like Wikipedia -- follow the footnotes

extracts.extract: ChatGPT can write code to deploy a machine learning model. And it can produce a set of sentences to train on. Flagging keywords is cool. Flagging ideas is cooler. Speed up big doc review, or set up alerts.
extracts.chapter: Hands-on with AI Tools for Journalists
extracts.notes: LLMs can generate training data and ML code for document classification

extracts.extract: If you disclose, put it in an editor's note at the start or a footnote at the end of the story. AI images or video should ALWAYS be labeled as illustrations produced with AI.
extracts.chapter: Hands-on with AI Tools for Journalists
extracts.notes: Reilley: always label AI-generated images; disclose AI use in editor's note

extracts.extract: LLMs can generate near-flawless data practices requests, properly citing state laws, rough template of a FOIA letter and sometimes include PIO and contact info. Build a data dictionary and design basic interactive templates. Generate whole R data dashboards, build a fast webpage, solve complex dataviz solutions, translate between data formats.
extracts.chapter: Hands-on with AI Tools for Journalists
extracts.notes: Reilley: comprehensive list of LLM capabilities for data journalism

extracts.extract: Provide Reference Text: Include reference materials for more reliable answers, especially on less-known topics. Allow Time for Thinking: Ask the AI to explain its thought process for better answers. Test, Test, and Retest: Keep throwing a mix of challenges at the AI. Assign it a role: Reporter, doctor, expert in a certain field.
extracts.chapter: Hands-on with AI Tools for Journalists
extracts.notes: Reilley: advanced prompting -- reference text, think-aloud, role personas, testing

extracts.extract: Datasette Cloud is a collaborative space for your newsroom to share and analyze data. Each organization gets a space -- a private space to collaborate on data. Philosophically, avoiding lockin is very important to us. You should be able to extract your data at any time, in an open format.
extracts.notes: Willison: Datasette Cloud as collaborative newsroom data platform; anti-lock-in

extracts.extract: Enrichments are a powerful new Datasette feature which allow you to run data modification operations against rows in a table. We'll use the regular expression enrichment to add a hireYear column: Source column: hireDate. Regular expression: (\d{4})-.*. Output column: hireYear.
extracts.notes: Datasette enrichments: apply regex transforms to columns via GUI

extracts.extract: datasette-extract is a brand new Datasette plugin that lets you enter unstructured data into a Datasette table using the OpenAI GPT-4 model. We're going to start an events calendar for the city, without any tedious data entry.
extracts.chapter: Extracting data into a table with AI
extracts.notes: Willison: datasette-extract uses GPT-4 to structure unstructured text into tables

extracts.extract: Work the cost-benefit, ChatGPT and the like are great, when they work. Flatten privilege -- attending NICAR is awesome, a masters is great. If you can't have that yet, AI can help fill some gaps in access. Think about your unique selling point -- if ChatGPT can be a decent data analyst and an improving writer, what makes you special?
extracts.notes: Ford: AI as access equalizer for under-resourced reporters; consider unique human value

extracts.extract: ChatGPT can be hit or miss as an attorney, but it can write clean Python (or other languages) faster than you can type. Excellent tutor, build on what you're starting at NICAR. You still need to be able to read the code. Can debug well if the code is relatively short.
extracts.notes: Ford: LLMs write code fast but you must read and verify; good for short code debugging

extracts.extract: newsroom guidelines, personal ethics, trained on proprietary or private data w/o permission, bias in models, risk of plagiarism, energy consumption for model training, transparency/passing off LLM content as human work, feeding an LLM proprietary info that becomes training data, use cases that reduce trust, supply chain ethics (RLHF; data sources)
extracts.notes: Kao: comprehensive taxonomy of AI ethical concerns for newsrooms

extracts.extract: Treat it like an intern -- always check its work. Generally, legalistic records requests get better responses. So let it be your pitbull (for informal purposes, and be mindful of security). Help draft the initial request, battle it out with citations.
extracts.notes: Ford: LLMs draft legal records requests but output requires human verification

extracts.extract: Visual debugging is typically the first thing I do when I'm working with a PDF for the first time. pdfplumber comes with a set of features for identifying and extracting tables on a given page. debug_tablefinder shows you what the table-finding method sees.
extracts.notes: Singer-Vine: always start with visual debugging; debug_tablefinder shows table detection

extracts.extract: Page.filter takes a function that will return True for objects we want to keep and False for those we want to ditch. Text and graphical objects can have two types of colors: stroking_color (the outline) and non_stroking_color (the fill). With text, you're almost always looking for the non_stroking_color.
extracts.notes: Singer-Vine: filter PDF objects by color/attribute for targeted text extraction

extracts.extract: I Do, We Do, You Do. Demonstrate a concept, go through it together, then give them plenty of time to experiment on their own while you and your coach walk around and answer questions. The pace will be slower than you think. Most people who come to this class will have zero experience with programming, so be empathetic.
extracts.notes: IRE teaching approach: I Do/We Do/You Do scaffolding for novice programmers

extracts.extract: If you can, find an opportunity when someone has gotten an error and take 5 minutes to walk through basic debugging strategy: Reading the traceback error from bottom to top, strategic Googling, etc.
extracts.notes: Teaching tip: demonstrate real-time debugging -- read tracebacks bottom-to-top

extracts.extract: By default, Python's third-party packages are all installed in a shared global folder. This approach is fine for your first experiments with Python, but it quickly falls apart when you start to get serious about coding. Programmers solve this problem by creating a virtual environment for each project that isolates them into discrete, independent containers.
extracts.notes: Virtual environments prevent dependency conflicts between projects

extracts.extract: Overview of textual data -- gold standard because most data programs can import/export, not proprietary. .csv -- most common; each piece of data is separated by a comma. .tsv or .tab -- tab-separated values. You'll get custom delimiters sometimes: pipe, tilde, semicolon.
extracts.chapter: Google Sheets: Importing and data prep
extracts.notes: Garcia: text-based formats are gold standard -- non-proprietary, universally importable

extracts.extract: Help your process, help your colleagues, add something interactive to your reporting, ROI is strong because it can be done fairly quickly and bears fruit for a long time.
extracts.notes: Ford: LLM-built web apps for reporting have high ROI -- quick to build, long-lasting value

extracts.extract: GPTExcel can turn even the most complex verbal descriptions into functional Excel formulas. Can provide straightforward explanations of what the formula is doing. Can choose Excel, Sheets or Airtable for specific syntax. Can do SQL, VBA, Regex and Google Scripts too.
extracts.chapter: Hands-on with AI Tools for Journalists
extracts.notes: Reilley: GPTExcel translates natural language to formulas across platforms

extracts.extract: native PDFs (text is selectable) vs. image PDFs (basically a picture of a piece of paper, requires Optical Character Recognition to extract text)
extracts.chapter: Google Sheets: Importing and data prep
extracts.notes: Garcia: critical distinction between native (searchable) and image PDFs for data extraction

extracts.extract: Assign it a role: 'You are a data journalist and need to...' Fill out two fields in the custom instructions pulldown menu. Tailor your results the way you want them. Helps ChatGPT better understand you, your needs and expectations. Tell it to cite sources, provide links and bulleted list of edits.
extracts.chapter: Hands-on with AI Tools for Journalists
extracts.notes: Reilley: custom instructions personalize LLM output; specify role and output preferences

extracts.extract: Some of the data sets in Austin have all the address parts crammed into a single field. Regex can easily explode that into individual columns. We are building a pattern in our regular expression field, creating a group to capture each part of the address that we want to keep. We'll continue the pattern outside the group until we get to the next part we want to keep, when we'll create a new group, and so on.
extracts.notes: Core pattern: build capture groups for each desired field, with non-captured separators between

extracts.extract: Take note for a minute at the number of matches at the top right: There are 325 matches, and that is how many records we should end up with. You'll want to keep referring back to that and making sure you have 325 groups.
extracts.notes: Use match counts as running validation while building regex patterns

extracts.extract: Now, take a moment and check how many matching groups we have. Wait ... WHOA ... we have only 314. What could've gone wrong? Scroll down the Test string until you find something amiss. The colors help you spot problems easily. BEE CAVE and WEST LAKE HILLS have spaces while AUSTIN and PFLUGERVILLE do not. It looks like our city group did not capture a needed space, which wasn't really revealed until we tried to carry out the pattern with the comma that is found after the city. This happens a lot … we need to back up and fix the error.
extracts.notes: Regex patterns frequently break on edge cases (spaces in city names); dropping match counts signal the error; visual inspection in regex101 helps locate failures

extracts.extract: Now that we have a pattern with our six groups of data, we can substitute them in any order we want using a search and replace, and we can build our substitution string right here in Regex101 as well. Our goal with the substitution string is to pull back our six groups, but to put tabs in between each of them. If we can build a search and replace like this, then we can paste the result back into Excel, and each group will become its own column.
extracts.notes: Tab-delimited substitution enables direct paste-to-spreadsheet workflow

extracts.extract: This can work fine for a small data set, but things bog down on the site if you have many rows or columns of data. I often use regex101 with sample text to figure out my patterns, but then use them in a text editor or within a R or Python script.
extracts.notes: Prototype regex patterns on sample data in regex101, then execute at scale in code editor or script

extracts.extract: When I want to clean a single column of data, I often download the data and will just copy out one column into my text editor and work it before pasting back the results into Excel, carefully making sure they still line up.
extracts.notes: Isolate a single column in a text editor for cleaning, then paste back — requires careful row alignment

extracts.extract: With regular expressions, you can search for a pattern of characters. Instead of searching just for 512-, you can look for 'three numbers together at the beginning of a line that are then followed by a dash'. If you capture that matching pattern as a group, you can then replace that group with parentheses outside it, no matter what the contents of the group.
extracts.notes: Core insight: regex replaces literal string matching with pattern-based matching, enabling batch transformations across heterogeneous values

extracts.extract: Windows and MacOS sometimes treat new line tokens differently. On a Mac, \n is a 'new line', but Windows uses \R. The regex101 editor will recognize either, and Visual Studio Code also seems to handle either, but if you are on Windows and have trouble, you might need to use \R instead of \n in some cases.
extracts.chapter: New lines differe between Mac and PCs
extracts.notes: Platform-specific newline tokens are a common source of regex failures when moving between OS

extracts.extract: And remember, if you are stumped by something, chances are you are not the first. Google and Stack Overflow are your friends. ChatGPT might be helpful, too, if you are good at prompts.

Reshaping data is considered a part of analyzing and visualizing data. Basically, you will process your data for a specific type of data visualization. Two approaches: 1. Convert data from a wide to a long format, or spread data (Unpivot Table) 2. Convert data from a long to a wide format, or summarize data (Pivot Table)
extracts.notes: Reshaping is framed as prerequisite to visualization; two canonical directions

extracts.extract: Normally, we reshape data from wide to long format when we have multiple columns, variables or measures that we want to consolidate or plot together.
extracts.notes: Decision rule for when wide-to-long is needed

extracts.extract: The =FLATTEN() function helps consolidate all the values from one or more data ranges into a single column. To hold the range of consolidated values returned from the FLATTEN() function, we need to use the =ARRAYFORMULA() function because it enables the display of values into multiple rows and/or columns.
extracts.notes: FLATTEN + ARRAYFORMULA is the Google Sheets approach to unpivoting

extracts.extract: If you need to split the newly created column into two columns, you can use the =SPLIT() function and wrap it around the =FLATTEN() function. Just make sure you wrap the above function inside the =ARRAYFORMULA() function.
extracts.notes: Three-function nesting pattern: ARRAYFORMULA(SPLIT(FLATTEN(...))) for multi-column unpivot

extracts.extract: Normally, we create a pivot table when we have data in a long format with multiple categories or groups in one column and we want to see the summary statistics for each category/group.
extracts.notes: Decision rule for when long-to-wide (pivot) is needed

extracts.extract: First, select the long table by placing your cursor inside it, then hold Cmd/Ctrl-A. Next, go to Insert > Pivot table > New sheet. Under Rows, add state. Under Columns, add data_updated. Under Values, add tot_deaths. Uncheck Show Totals.
extracts.notes: Step-by-step pivot table creation in Google Sheets for COVID-19 data

extracts.extract: ID columns refer to the columns in the long table that you want to keep and not spread in the flipped table. Using a Pivot table in Google Sheets may be the fastest and most efficient way to flip data from a long to a wide format. It works even when you have multiple ID columns in your datatable.
extracts.chapter: The case of multiple ID columns
extracts.notes: Pivot tables handle multi-column ID cases that formula approaches struggle with

extracts.extract: Apps Script can be used to create custom functions interacting with your Google Sheet files and the data they contain. In this session, we will adopt and revise the free scripts written by Robin Gertenbach, a data scientist working for Google. Using scripts is a faster way to reshape data. These scripts will generate two custom functions melt(), to reshape data from wide to long, and cast(), to reshape data from long to wide. You just need to set up the scripts for one Google Sheets template, and you can make a copy and reuse it for multiple reshaping tasks.
extracts.notes: Custom Apps Script functions melt/cast extend Google Sheets with R/Python-style reshape capabilities

extracts.extract: To deal with large datasets, you need a more powerful platform or a programming language such as Python and R outside of Google Sheets. A convenient platform for you to write Python/R code to reshape data and write the output back to Google Sheets is Google Colab.
extracts.notes: Google Sheets formula/script approaches hit limits; Colab provides Python/R for larger datasets with Sheets integration

extracts.extract: First, you need to upload your data to Google Drive, create a new spreadsheet in Google Sheets, or upload your data to Colab directly. Then, connect Google Colab to Google Sheets using the gspread package. Third, import the pandas package and use the pivot() function to flip data from a long to a wide format. To flip data from a wide to a long format, use the melt() function. Fourth, write the new dataframe back to your Google Sheets.
extracts.chapter: How to reshape data in Colab?
extracts.notes: Four-step Colab workflow: upload → connect via gspread → pandas pivot/melt → write back to Sheets

extracts.extract: Google Sheets can handle up to 10 million cells or 18,278 columns (column ZZZ). The Apps Script we added to Google Sheets can't flip long tables of the third and fourth datasets because both are too large for the script to handle.
extracts.notes: Hard limits on Google Sheets capacity constrain spreadsheet-based reshaping

extracts.extract: Useful applications of text analysis: Organizing documents, Exploratory exercise, Data analysis. Tips: Keep it simple. Use available resources. Research the backdrop of these resources. Automation still requires manual verification.
extracts.chapter: Text analysis in different languages for investigations
extracts.notes: Four guiding principles for text analysis in journalism; emphasis on manual verification of automated results

extracts.extract: My favorite techniques: Entity extraction (Named Entity Recognition), Identify themes (Topic Modeling), Label types of words (Part Of Speech Tagging), Split text into smaller units (Tokenization: sentences / words), Find the base form of a word (Lemmatization), Resolve relationships between entities (Coreference Resolution), Find information based on the meaning of the query (Semantic Search).
extracts.chapter: Text analysis in different languages for investigations
extracts.notes: Seven core NLP techniques for investigative journalism

extracts.extract: Tools: DocumentCloud (MuckRock), Aleph (OCCRP), Datashare (ICIJ), Pinpoint (Google). Python libraries: Spacy, Stanza, NLTK, Transformers, BERTopic.
extracts.chapter: Text analysis in different languages for investigations
extracts.notes: Two tiers of text analysis tools: GUI platforms for document management, Python libraries for custom NLP

extracts.extract: Your automated analysis is only going to be as good as your OCR.
extracts.notes: OCR quality is the bottleneck for all downstream text analysis

extracts.extract: Upload, Extract, Analyze, Search, Annotate, Redact, Edit, Embed & Publish, Collaborate, Add-Ons. Web upload, Batch upload CLI, Scraper Add-On. OCR, Entity extraction, Table extraction add-ons, Regex Add-On.
extracts.notes: DocumentCloud workflow covers full pipeline from upload through publication; add-ons extend core functionality

extracts.extract: There's an Add-On for that. Some of my favorites: Transcribe Audio (Whisper), Klaxon, GPT 3.5 Turbo Playground, Custom metadata scraper, Bad Redactions, Scraper.
extracts.notes: DocumentCloud add-ons include AI transcription, LLM analysis, and automated redaction detection

extracts.extract: Image to Text: Optical Character Recognition (OCR) — Tesseract, in R and Python. Audio to Text: Automated Transcription/Speech-to-Text — Whisper (OpenAI-WhisperGUI).
extracts.notes: Two modality conversion pipelines: image→text via Tesseract, audio→text via Whisper

extracts.extract: Language Detection: R: {textcat} (~60 languages), Python: {langdetect} (~50 languages), {polyglot} (+100 languages), Google Sheets: =DETECTLANGUAGE(). Language Translation: R: {translateR}, Deepl, Python: {polyglot}, {deepl-python}, Google Sheets: =GOOGLETRANSLATE().
extracts.notes: Toolkit for multilingual analysis spanning R, Python, and Google Sheets; library coverage varies from 50 to 100+ languages

extracts.extract: Finding non-English resources: What Language (e.g., 'Bahasa Indonesia', 'Portuguese'). Low-Resource Languages vs. High-Resource Languages. What Task: Tokenizer, Stemmer, Lemmatizer, 'Part of Speech' tagger. What Type (e.g., R, Python, open source, user interface). Example search: Indonesian tokenizer Python.
extracts.notes: Three-axis search framework for finding NLP tools: language × task × implementation type

extracts.extract: A corpus (plural corpora) or text corpus is a large and structured set of texts. Tokenizing is the process of splitting your corpus into subsections: phrases, sentences, words. The goal of both stemming and lemmatization is to reduce inflectional forms and sometimes derivationally related forms of a word to a common base form. Stemming usually refers to a crude heuristic process that chops off the ends of words. Lemmatization usually refers to doing things properly with the use of a vocabulary and morphological analysis of words, normally aiming to remove inflectional endings only and to return the base or dictionary form of a word.
extracts.notes: Core NLP vocabulary: corpus → tokenize → stem/lemmatize; lemmatization is more precise but computationally heavier than stemming

extracts.extract: Copy the Regular Expression pattern you built in Regex101 and insert into the Find text field in VS Code. Copy the Substitution pattern and insert it into the Replace field. Click on the button on the far-right of the Find line, the one that has .* in it. Now, hit the 'Replace All' button to the far-right of the Replace field. Once you run the search and replace, you can copy and paste the results into a spreadsheet and it will be six distinct columns.
extracts.notes: Regex101-to-VS-Code workflow: prototype pattern online, then execute in editor with .* regex mode enabled

extracts.extract: We want to split these six distinct pieces into their own columns for each record. Why? Many reasons, but one is to use the latitude and longitude for data visualizations. We will build a Regular Expression pattern to Find and capture six groups of text and then Replace those groups with tabs between them so we can put them back into a spreadsheet as columns.
extracts.notes: Defines the purpose of field splitting: enable downstream geocoding and visualization by separating lat/long from composite address fields

extracts.extract: Many government agencies use Socrata as their open data portal: From Austin to Boston; from Los Angeles to New York.
extracts.notes: Socrata is a widespread government open data platform; familiarity with its data formats is broadly applicable

extracts.extract: A regular expression (regex or regexp for short) is a special text string for describing a search pattern. Searching text with a pattern instead for a specific string of characters can be VERY useful for cleaning data. The concept is available in many programming languages and tools. While we'll use regex101.com to learn the syntax, that site can handle only so much data. In practice, I tend to use regex101 to test my patterns on sample data, but then execute them in a spreadsheet formula, code editor or in a programming language like R or Python.
extracts.notes: Regex is a universal data cleaning skill transferable across tools; prototype on samples, execute in production environment

extracts.extract: You can actually save this example in regex101 under SAVE & SHARE in the top left panel. If you create a login with regex101 you can keep a dashboard of them.
extracts.notes: Building a personal regex pattern library in regex101 for reuse

extracts.extract: We need to use something called a 'character set' that allows us to include more than one token within a group. We signify this by putting what we want inside of square brackets: So we need to put the \w inside square brackets along with the space so we can catch both.
extracts.notes: Character sets (square brackets) are the fix for patterns that need to match heterogeneous character types

extracts.extract: The Washington Post published a racing line chart on March 1, 2023. Harry Stevens, the reporter and designer, provides his data source and codebook at this link. Estimates for every country's carbon dioxide emissions since 1750 was gathered from The Global Carbon Project.
extracts.notes: Example of journalist providing source data and codebook alongside published visualization

extracts.extract: wide_df = df.pivot(index=['Country', 'ISO 3166-1 alpha-3','UN M49'], columns='Year', values='Total')
extracts.notes: pandas pivot() with multi-column index for preserving ID columns during reshape
